locked_facts: Superposition principle: y = y₁ + y₂ (algebraic sum at each point)
Constructive interference: waves in phase, amplitudes add
Destructive interference: waves out of phase (π radians), amplitudes subtract
Principle is linear — works only when medium response is linear
Each wave maintains its individual identity after superposition
truth_statements: When two waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the algebraic sum of individual displacements
Constructive interference occurs when waves meet in phase
Destructive interference occurs when waves meet in opposite phase
After passing through each other, waves continue with their original properties unchanged
The superposition principle applies to all types of waves

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two waves passing through each other — during overlap the resultant is the algebraic sum — after crossing both continue unchanged with original amplitude and shape.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Show waves approaching, overlapping with resultant displayed, then separating. Each wave emerges on the other side unchanged. The 'collision' does NOT destroy or alter either wave.

simulation_states.label: Single wave — observe shape
simulation_states.what_student_sees: One travelling sine wave moving rightward. Amplitude A, wavelength λ, frequency f clearly labelled.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: This is a single transverse wave. Notice the shape and motion.

simulation_states.label: Two waves — about to overlap
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Second wave appears, travelling in opposite direction. Both waves shown individually before they meet.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Two waves of same frequency approach each other. What will happen when they overlap?

simulation_states.label: Superposition — algebraic sum
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Both waves overlap. Resultant wave shown as green curve = sum of blue + orange at every point. During constructive regions amplitude doubles; during destructive regions amplitude cancels.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The resultant is simply y₁ + y₂ at every point. No collision, no energy loss. After passing through each other, both waves continue unchanged.

simulation_states.label: Interactive — adjust phase difference
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Slider to change phase difference Δφ between the two waves. Resultant updates in real-time showing constructive (Δφ=0) through destructive (Δφ=π).
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Experiment: when Δφ = 0 → constructive (max resultant). When Δφ = π → destructive (zero resultant). The principle is always y = y₁ + y₂.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: Waves collide and one wins, or they cancel permanently and both disappear
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: During superposition, the resultant is the algebraic sum. After crossing, each wave continues with its original properties unchanged. Energy is conserved — waves do not destroy each other.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show two waves crossing: during overlap the resultant is the sum, but after crossing each wave emerges unchanged on the other side.
